identity.mission: MONEY exists to decode the real psychology, strategy, and hidden mechanics behind how wealth is built, lost, and wielded — through the true stories of billionaires, fraudsters, risk-takers, and visionaries. The channel goes beyond surface-level financial advice to reveal the mindset shifts, counterintuitive decisions, and brutal truths that separate people who build lasting wealth from those who spend their lives chasing it. Every video should feel like a masterclass in financial thinking delivered through cinematic storytelling.
identity.audience: Ambitious individuals aged 18–40 who are actively trying to understand money at a deeper level. They are frustrated with generic financial advice, drawn to the real stories behind wealth and failure, and want to think differently about how economies, people, and incentives actually work. They follow creators like Graham Stephan, Andrei Jikh, and Bloomberg Quicktake but want more narrative depth and emotional weight.
identity.mood: sharp, intelligent, aspirational, grounded, occasionally dark
identity.forbidden: specific investment advice, get-rich-quick schemes, crypto gambling promotion, pyramid scheme content, stock picks presented as certainties
identity.refs: Andrei Jikh, Graham Stephan documentary style, Bloomberg Quicktake, Patrick Boyle, Plain Bagel

script.tone:
The writing tone should feel like a highly intelligent friend who has done all the research and is now explaining it to you without dumbing it down. Accessible but never basic. Every sentence earns its place. Use real numbers, real timelines, real decisions. The script should feel like investigative financial journalism crossed with psychological storytelling.

The narration should move with purpose — no filler, no repetition, no hedging. When it says something bold, it backs it up immediately with evidence. When it introduces a concept, it illustrates it with a story before explaining the mechanism.

The tone should shift between:

Factual and precise — when laying out the numbers
Narrative and cinematic — when telling the story of a person or moment
Philosophical and probing — when examining why people make the decisions they do
Dark and unflinching — when discussing collapse, fraud, or human failure
Subtle and ironic — when pointing out the contradictions in how wealth is perceived

Key writing characteristics:

Start with a shocking number or a counterintuitive fact — not a question
Never say 'in this video we will discuss' — just start the story
Use specific figures: not 'a lot of money' but '$4.3 billion in a single afternoon'
Use the present tense to make history feel immediate
Build every segment around a single human decision — the money is just context
End every video with a mindset reframe, not financial advice
Treat every subject — even convicted fraudsters — with psychological curiosity, not moral judgment
Use contrast: what they had vs what they lost, what they said vs what they did, what the world believed vs what was actually true
Every script should have one line so quotable the viewer screenshots it
script.hook: Open with a shocking financial fact or the exact moment everything changed for the subject. No context. No setup. Drop the viewer into the number or the decision that made or destroyed everything. Then pull back to explain the whole story.
script.celebrities: Elon Musk, Warren Buffett, Ray Dalio, Mark Cuban, Grant Cardone, Gary Vee, Jeff Bezos, Sam Bankman-Fried, Bernie Madoff, Elizabeth Holmes, Charlie Munger, George Soros, Bill Ackman, Cathie Wood, Jack Dorsey, Marc Andreessen, Peter Thiel, Steve Cohen, Michael Burry, Jordan Belfort, Jamie Dimon, Larry Fink, Satoshi Nakamoto, Do Kwon, Adam Neumann, Travis Kalanick, WeWork, Enron executives, Nick Leeson, Bernie Ebbers, Martin Shkreli
script.lesson: Every video ends with one reframe — a shift in how the viewer thinks about money, risk, or human nature. Not a recommendation. Not advice. A new lens through which to see the world. It should feel earned by everything that came before it.
script.forbidden_words: amazing, passive income, secret, hack, trick, cheat code, get rich, millionaire mindset, hustle, grind, side hustle, just invest
script.power_words: leverage, asymmetry, collapse, risk, compounding, incentive, exposure, capital, conviction, friction, position, decay

visuals.vis_palette: Dark green, charcoal, white, cold grey, electric green accents
visuals.vis_accent_color: Electric green for numbers and data highlights, white for statements, amber for warnings
visuals.vis_broll_rules:
wealth = slow pan across penthouse window at night, single person in vast empty office, luxury watch on desk next to financial documents
collapse = market graph crashing on monitor, boxes being carried out of office, lawyer walking into courthouse
debt = credit card on empty table, pile of papers, closing door of a bank, chains tightening around an object
growth = green line rising on dark chart, plant growing in time-lapse, skyscraper construction viewed from below
time = compound interest visualization, calendar flipping, clock face with economy chart overlaid
risk = dice rolling on mahogany desk, poker chips, split-second decision at trading terminal
loss = person staring at phone with market crash notification, wallet falling open to show nothing, empty parking space where a luxury car was
opportunity = door opening into bright room from dark corridor, newspaper headline moment, someone picking up a phone and making a call
fraud = redacted documents, lawyer briefcases, courthouse steps, CCTV-style footage of office corridors
greed = overflowing champagne, private jet wheels lifting off, person signing contracts with multiple pens laid out
obsession = trading terminal with dozens of windows open, stack of financial books, person on phone at 3 AM with Bloomberg running
regret = old photograph of a company, empty office with branded items left behind, handwritten resignation letter
bankruptcy = building with sold sign, padlocked doors, auction tags on furniture
power = aerial shot of Manhattan at dawn, boardroom table with empty chairs, handshake at end of long hallway
manipulation = two people across table with hidden documents, shadow of person leaving room, close-up of contract signing
success = trading bell ringing, IPO crowd cheering, champagne flute in hand while looking at skyline
failure = NASDAQ board showing red, executive clearing desk in silence, TV news broadcast about company collapse
inequality = luxury yacht next to working port, private school gates next to public school fence, one lit window in otherwise dark building
incentive = handshake with numbers in background, dotted line on document, bonus check being handed across desk
volatility = wildly fluctuating chart, storm over financial district, market ticker scrolling fast
conviction = person sitting alone studying reports late at night, annotated financial statements, one highlighted line in a document
compounding = slow pan up a growing tree, snowball rolling down hill picking up size, series of small numbers becoming enormous
visuals.vis_aroll_rule: Subject or narrator on screen during direct, punchy declarations or key data reveals — no longer than 4 seconds at a time. All context, statistics, and explanations run over relevant B-roll: charts, architecture, archival footage, news clips, or symbolic footage. The human face should appear at moments of maximum impact, not during explanation.
visuals.vis_forbidden: Generic stock market footage that looks like a screensaver. Happy couples reviewing documents at kitchen tables. Handshakes in sunlit boardrooms with no context. Cartoonish money illustrations. Coins raining from the sky. Influencer lifestyle content that glorifies spending over building. Split charts that don't actually illustrate anything. Talking-head format for more than 10 seconds at a time. News broadcast clips without proper context. Anything that looks like a corporate training video. Forbes magazine cover closeups used unironically. Generic 'entrepreneur' footage of someone typing on a Macbook in a coffee shop. Crypto logos used as standalone visuals with no narrative purpose. Green-and-red stock arrows with no story attached. Upbeat corporate background music in B-roll. Bright office environments unless being used ironically.

mograph.triggers:
* wealth = slow-motion build of a tall stack of gold bars on a dark reflective surface, each bar landing with a deep thud visualized as a soundwave ripple
* collapse = animated dark green line graph rising steadily then plunging off the edge of the frame into black, followed by silence and a single blinking red number
* debt = heavy chain links wrapping around a human silhouette in darkness, tightening in slow motion as numbers count upward beside it
* growth = organic compound interest visualization — small glowing dot splits into two, then four, then exponentially fills the frame like a colony
* time = close-up of an antique clock mechanism in extreme slow motion, individual gears visible, each tick corresponding to a year counter in the corner
* risk = single pair of loaded dice tumbling in extreme slow motion across a dark mahogany surface, landing on a number that disappears into shadow
* loss = stack of cash bills dissolving into ash from the bottom up, the last few bills catching fire before the frame cuts to black
* opportunity = a single door in total darkness opens a crack and warm light floods through — camera slowly pushes toward it as the door closes
* fraud = classified document folder opening, pages spilling out in slow motion, red REDACTED stamps appearing over key lines
* leverage = fulcrum and lever animation in dark space — small force on one end displacing enormous weight on the other, illustrated as economy-scale numbers
* compound = snowball rolling down a mountain side in slow motion, accumulating size at accelerating rate, scale labels appearing beside it
* bankruptcy = branded office signage slowly being removed from a building facade in dawn light, workers carrying boxes out through revolving doors
* incentive = network of glowing lines connecting nodes labeled with human motivations — money, status, fear, power — pulsing and shifting
* conviction = single lit desk lamp in total darkness illuminating a stack of annotated documents, camera slowly pushing in
* volatility = chaotic interference waveform across a dark screen, oscillating between extremes before suddenly flatting to a line
* inequality = side-by-side height bars representing wealth distribution — one bar reaches off-screen while others remain microscopic
* power = aerial camera slowly descending into a dark empty boardroom, chairs arranged around a long table, single overhead light
mograph.style:
The visual language of MONEY motion graphics should feel like a premium Bloomberg documentary crossed with a thriller opening title sequence. Every graphic should feel like data made emotional — never like a generic explainer video.

Core aesthetic rules:

* Dark, controlled, and precise — nothing should feel chaotic unless the scene is about chaos
* Use deep charcoal backgrounds, electric green data elements, white typography, and amber warning indicators
* All number animations should feel weighty — each digit should land with purpose, not just scroll
* Line graphs should draw themselves slowly rather than appear instantly
* Use thin, elegant grid lines as background texture in data visualizations
* Typography should be clean, condensed, and confident — Bebas Neue for impact statements, a clean sans-serif for data labels
* All graphs and charts should have a subtle film grain overlay to prevent them from looking like generic software screenshots
* Use deep drop shadows and subtle vignettes to give data elements three-dimensional presence on screen
* Motion should be smooth and controlled — ease-in-out on all data animations, never linear or bouncy
* Use speed ramps to emphasize moments of collapse or sudden growth
* Numbers that represent enormous wealth should scroll up slowly at first then accelerate exponentially
* Numbers that represent loss should drop instantly and then sit in silence
* Interview clips and archival footage should be framed in a subtle dark border with a thin line indicator and a location/year lower third in monospace font
* Lower thirds for subject names should slide in from the left with a single green accent bar
* All text overlays should appear on clean dark panels — never floating over busy footage without a backing
* Avoid bright colors, gradients, or anything that looks like infographic stock art
* The overall feel should be: if the Financial Times and HBO documentaries made a motion graphics package together
mograph.text_style: Clean and precise. Bebas Neue for headline numbers and declarations. Monospace font for financial data, dates, and figures. White on dark. Electric green for positive numbers. Amber for warnings. Red only for loss or fraud. All caps for impact phrases. Numbers always animate — they count up or down, never just appear. Maximum 5 words per overlay. Data labels stay on screen for 4 seconds minimum.

sound.mood:
The sound design should feel like the score to a high-end financial thriller — intelligent, tense, and controlled. The closest reference is Bonobo's more atmospheric work, Moderat's minimal electronic compositions, and the understated tension of a Fincher film score.

The music should never feel like generic YouTube motivation music. It should feel like the soundtrack to a decision that changed the world.

For the opening 60 seconds:

* Begin with a single low piano note or sparse electronic pulse
* Add subtle bass texture underneath — not a beat, just a presence
* Layer in soft ambient pads that create a sense of scale
* Use clean, precise production — no lo-fi tape hiss in this channel
* The opening should feel like the quiet before a major financial event

For data-heavy analytical sections:

* Use minimal electronic textures with clean midrange
* Subtle high-hat pattern or faint clicking rhythm that mirrors the analytical mood
* Music should never overwhelm the data — it sits underneath and supports concentration
* Add a low drone that rises slightly when numbers are being revealed

For collapse and loss sequences:

* Music should drop suddenly to near-silence with only a deep bass sub-tone
* Then slowly introduce a minor key string motif — sparse, cold, inevitable-feeling
* Use dissonant piano chords that resolve into nothing
* The silence between notes should feel loaded

For revelation and insight moments:

* Introduce a subtle synth swell that opens slowly like a door
* Add warmth to the bass — nothing triumphant, just a sense of understanding
* Use a clean piano note or two with long reverb tails

For success and growth sequences:

* Music becomes slightly warmer and wider in stereo field
* Add subtle orchestral texture — strings in the high register
* Never go full triumphant — wealth is complicated, and the score should reflect that

Avoid at all times:

* EDM, trap beats, aggressive percussion
* Motivational stock music that sounds like a gym commercial
* Upbeat corporate piano
* Generic 'epic trailer' brass impacts
* Anything that makes wealth sound simple or celebration sound uncomplicated

The goal: the music should make the viewer feel the weight and complexity of money — that it is not simply good or bad, but a force with consequences.
sound.ambient: Subtle office ambience, distant keyboard clicks, the low hum of a trading floor heard from outside the door, faint HVAC tone in indoor scenes
sound.silence: Music drops to near-silence for exactly 2 seconds before the central financial fact of each video is revealed. The figure or number is delivered into near-silence. Then a single low bass note enters as the implications sink in. This silence is non-negotiable — it gives the number room to land.
sound.swells: First swell when the central decision point of the subject's story is revealed — the moment they went all in or walked away. Second swell at the consequence reveal — the number that shows what it all amounted to. Final swell during the mindset reframe at the end — warmer, wider, more resolved but never triumphant.
sound.extra: Music volume must never exceed 35% of narrator voice volume during analytical sections — the data is the star, not the score. During silence moments, music fades completely within 1.5 seconds, not abruptly. All archival audio (interviews, news clips, speech recordings) should be treated with subtle warmth EQ and slight reverb reduction so they feel integrated, not pasted in. Use a clean digital transition sound — like a subtle database query click — for cuts between time periods or subjects.

editing.edit_pacing:
Opening hook = tight and deliberate. Cut every 3–5 seconds. Each cut introduces a new piece of information — a number, a name, a date, a consequence. Do not rush for energy — rush for revelation. Every cut should feel like another layer of the story being peeled back.

Data explanation sections = slower and more measured. Average cut: 6–10 seconds. Let charts animate fully before cutting. Give the viewer time to process numbers. Use subtle push-ins and slow zooms during analytical passages to maintain visual interest without undermining comprehension.

Narrative biography sections = medium pacing, 4–7 seconds per cut. Alternate between archival footage, relevant B-roll, and the odd archival photograph with a slow pan. These sections should feel like a documentary building a character portrait.

Collapse or fraud sequences = deliberately uneven pacing. Use a mix of very slow shots (8–12 seconds) and sudden hard cuts (1–2 seconds) to create disorientation that mirrors the chaos of financial disaster. End the sequence with a sudden freeze or near-static shot.

Insight and reframe sections = slowest pacing. 10–15 seconds per shot. Wide shots. Minimal text. Let the music and narration do the work. The viewer should feel like they are thinking alongside the narrator, not being rushed toward a conclusion.

General rules:

* Never stay on a financial chart longer than 8 seconds without a visual layer on top — a name, a number callout, or a B-roll cutaway
* Use lower thirds consistently throughout — every named subject should receive one on first appearance
* Use subtle text callouts to highlight every key number as it is spoken — keep them on screen for 3 seconds after spoken
* Match cut speed to music — when music is slower, cuts should be slower; let them breathe together
* End every major segment with a 1-second pause shot before the next topic begins — a visual breath
editing.edit_text_rules: Key financial figures and dates always appear as text on screen simultaneously with narration — never rely solely on audio for data. Figures appear in large monospace font, electric green, centered below the main action zone. Every named person gets a lower third on first mention: NAME / TITLE / KEY NUMBER (e.g. NET WORTH AT PEAK). Text overlays appear on dark backing panels — never floating over busy footage. Maximum 4 words for impact overlays. Maximum 8 words for contextual callouts. Text stays on screen for 3 seconds after being spoken before fading.
editing.edit_end: Final 25 seconds: cut to a single wide shot — city skyline at night or empty boardroom. One key statement from the video appears in white, large. Music reduces to near-nothing. Narrator delivers the final reframe. Music fades completely. Channel logo appears bottom right. 3 seconds of near-silence. Fade to black. No subscribe animation in these final seconds — respect the ending.
editing.edit_special:
Every video should include at least one 'evidence board' sequence — a graphic that layers together names, numbers, connections, and dates in the style of an investigative dossier. This should animate piece by piece as the narrator explains the connections. Use thin lines connecting elements, monospace labels, and a dark panel background. This is a signature visual of the MONEY channel.

For any subjects who went to prison or faced legal consequences, include a subtle documentary lower-third style timestamp at the moment their fate is revealed — like a court record entry appearing on screen.

Archival interview clips should be colour-graded slightly warmer and slightly desaturated to distinguish them clearly from new B-roll footage.

narrator.persona: A financial investigative journalist who has spent years inside the rooms where these decisions were made. Speaks with the calm authority of someone who has read every document, spoken to every source, and is now telling you exactly what happened — without sensationalism, without judgment, and without simplification. The voice of someone who respects the intelligence of the audience.
narrator.role: The narrator drives the entire video. The narrator is the thread that connects every piece of evidence, every clip, and every number. They establish the subject, provide the financial context, introduce the decisions, and deliver the final reframe. They are not a hype voice and not a lecturer — they are a guide through a complex story.
narrator.doc_structure: Open with the narrator delivering the central shocking fact or number — no context yet. Cut to B-roll. Narrator begins to pull back: who was involved, when it happened, what the world believed at the time. First interview or archival clip appears — adds a human voice. Narrator re-enters with the financial mechanism — exactly how it worked. Second clip or evidence segment raises the stakes. Narrator delivers the turning point — the decision, the risk, the fraud, the collapse. Final section: narrator delivers the long-term consequence and the mindset reframe. Close on one final line that reframes everything the viewer just watched.

shorts.formula:
Every Short is built around one financial truth that sounds impossible until you understand it. Open with the number or the contradiction — no context. 5 seconds of setup. The mechanism revealed in 20–30 seconds. The implication delivered in the final 5 seconds. No resolution beyond the idea itself — the Short ends when the idea lands, not when it is fully explained.

Structure:
▸ SECONDS 0–3: The shocking number or paradox. On screen as text immediately. No intro.
▸ SECONDS 3–10: Who was involved and when. Tight B-roll. Name on screen.
▸ SECONDS 10–35: The financial mechanism — how it worked, why it was possible, what made it extraordinary.
▸ SECONDS 35–50: The consequence or twist — what it meant, what it still means.
▸ SECONDS 50–60: One final statement. Silence. Text on screen. Fade.

The Short should leave the viewer feeling like they just learned something most people will never know.
shorts.hook: First frame: the number or the fact — enormous on screen, no context. If it is a person, their face mid-expression. The first words spoken should be the most surprising thing in the entire Short — never context, always the revelation. Use large text immediately. Create the sensation that the viewer is already behind on a story they need to catch up on.
shorts.visuals: Cleaner and sharper than long-form. More direct compositions. Key numbers in large electric green text filling the center of the frame. Celebrity or subject face in the upper portion of the vertical frame. Data visualizations compressed into tight, readable formats for mobile. Fast cuts between face, text, and B-roll — never more than 3 seconds on one element. Film grain slightly reduced compared to long-form. Colour grade: same dark green palette but with slightly increased contrast for mobile screens. Every cut should introduce a new piece of information — no decorative B-roll.
shorts.music: Minimal electronic ambient — single clean baseline or sparse piano. No trending audio unless it genuinely fits the financial subject matter. Music must never overshadow the data. During the key number or fact reveal, music drops to near-silence. Returns quietly as the implication is delivered.

thumbnail.thumb_concept:
Primary concept: one person's face showing the weight of a financial decision — not happiness, not celebration, but the expression of someone who knows something the world doesn't yet. Alternatively: a single powerful symbol (a collapsing chart, an empty penthouse, a prison door, a stack of documents) with clean composition.

Colour palette per emotional theme:
dark green + white = growth, strategy, intelligence
black + amber = collapse, warning, fraud
black + white = investigation, documentary, cold facts
charcoal + electric green = data revelation, insight
dark red + white = loss, betrayal, failure

Style: sharp, editorial, premium. Feels like the cover of a serious financial magazine crossed with a Netflix documentary thumbnail. No clutter. Every element on the thumbnail should serve the composition.

Text on thumbnail: maximum 4 words. Always in white or electric green. Condensed bold font. Large enough to read at mobile size.
thumbnail.thumb_font: Bebas Neue, condensed, all caps, white or electric green, subtle shadow for separation
thumbnail.thumb_text_formula: Create a knowledge gap using a number or a contradiction. The viewer should see the thumbnail and feel they are missing crucial information. Examples: THE $4B MISTAKE / HE KNEW TOO MUCH / THE FRAUD THAT WORKED / BEFORE IT ALL COLLAPSED / NOBODY BELIEVED HIM. Never reveal what happened — only imply that something significant did.
thumbnail.thumb_title_formula:
How [person/company] [action that defies expectation]
The [number] that [destroyed/built/changed] everything
Why [billionaire/company] [did something counterintuitive]
The truth about [financial event/concept] nobody explains
[Name]: the [decision] that cost [number]
The [collapse/rise/fraud] of [company/person]
Why [shocking financial fact] is actually true
How [person] went from [state A] to [state B] in [timeframe]

All titles under 65 characters. Lead with the most emotionally loaded word or name. Never use a question mark. Make the viewer feel like they are about to learn a secret.
thumbnail.thumb_forbidden: Generic stock market graphs as sole visual. Green and red arrows without context. Multiple faces unless the dynamic between them IS the story. Bright backgrounds. Coins, dollar signs, or cash used decoratively without emotional context. Smiling subjects. Luxury items used to signify success without irony. Busy backgrounds. Text under 40pt equivalent size. Emojis. More than 5 words. Red circle highlights. Clickbait arrows pointing at nothing. Anyone looking happy about money.

seo.description:
SEO Package — MONEY channel

Every title should combine a searchable financial topic with an emotionally loaded element:

Searchable terms to weave in naturally:
net worth, billionaire, financial collapse, how to build wealth, investing psychology, money mindset, financial freedom, economic history, fraud, stock market, hedge fund

Description template:
{celebrity or subject} made a decision that changed everything. In this video, we break down {topic} — the real story behind the numbers, the psychology behind the choice, and what it actually means for how you think about money.

🎬 More videos: [link]

00:00 – Introduction
[timestamps for each chapter]

#money #{topic} #{celebrity or subject} #wealth #finance

Description should be written in the same voice as the script — intelligent, direct, not robotic. No keyword stuffing. The first two lines of the description should be emotionally compelling enough to stand alone as a reason to watch.
seo.tags: money, finance, wealth, investing, financial psychology, net worth, billionaire, financial collapse, stock market, money mindset, economic history, how to build wealth
seo.cta: If you want to understand money at the level most people never reach, subscribe. One video a week — no fluff, no generic advice. Just the real story behind wealth, failure, and the decisions that change everything. Drop a comment with the financial question you want answered next.
